text: She didn’t want his trade, didn’t want his help, didn’t seem to want anything to do with his kind . But the emotion in her posture triggered hidden processes within him. He left his cover and jogged toward her. Before long, the road would be submerged completely. She wasn’t safe here, and neither was he . The cloth around her head was askew, and a few wild strands of hair had slipped out of it. The rubble gathered at the storm drain in front of her was acting as a small dam, creating a growing pool around her; she didn’t appear to notice . Ronin stopped behind the woman. She didn’t move save the slow, shaky rise and fall of her shoulders. Her soft sobs were barely audible over the storm . “Are you well?” he asked. It was normal for humans to cry, wasn’t it? Especially after a fall like she’d taken ? The woman stiffened, lifted her head, and looked over her shoulder. Her reddened eyes met his optics. She turned toward him with surprising speed, pushing back on hands and feet. Her skirts bunched between her thighs and floated on the murky current, revealing her pale, shapely legs . She pulled a knife from her thigh and jabbed it into the air between them . “ You !” “Have been as long as I can remember .” “Why are you following me?” With her free hand, she wiped moisture from her face as she stood. She kept the knife up, as though it were the thing holding Ronin at bay . “I’m a dustwalker. My business takes me out of town frequently .” “This isn’t the Dust .” “No. But all roads lead to it.” He almost said more, but he wanted to put her at ease. Speaking about death wouldn’t help . “Then go,” she said, wagging the knife toward the south . Ronin tilted his head, studying her. The woman’s tone was firm, voice strong, but her eyes were red, the flesh around them slightly swollen. She pressed her lips together, but it didn’t hide their trembling. Her grip on the weapon was white- knuckled . “Are you well?” he asked again, adjusting the modulation of his voice to a gentler tone . “I’ll be fine, once you’re gone.” She took a single step backward. The water streamed around her ankles like the rapids of a river. Cheyenne was a thirsty place, but it could rarely handle anything beyond a drizzle . He lifted his optics, up over one of her exposed legs. There was a scrape on her knee. A superficial wound, most likely, but organics were susceptible to many diseases and infections that could prove fatal . “You’re bleeding,” he said . “I’ll. Be . Fine .” Ronin frowned. He’d seen no small amount of blood during his existence. There was no reason for her blood to concern him, but the idea of her in pain was unsettling. “I can dress your wound .” “What the hell do you want from me?” She demanded, voice breaking into a higher pitch . What did he want? Why had he entered the human settlement with the first light of dawn and followed this woman out here, only to watch her from hiding? Why finally approach her ? There was no logic behind anything he’d done or felt regarding this woman . Moving slowly, he shrugged off his pack and brought it to his front. The woman took another cautious step back as he unfastened the flap. Reaching in with a bare-metal hand — her eyes dropped to it and widened — he withdrew the bundle he’d purchased from the food vendor . Her wary gaze darted between his face and the paper-wrapped item in his hand. Ronin extended his arm, holding it out to her. She stared, moving no closer. The rain fell loudly on the paper . “If you’re hesitating because nothing’s free, I’ll barter with you for this .” “I-I told you, I don’t trade with bots.” Her lower lip quivered . Ronin shouldered his pack to free his other hand. Taking the corner of the paper between forefinger and thumb, he peeled it back, giving her a fleeting glimpse of the spiced, dried meat inside . “I have a proposal to make,” he said, folding the paper closed. He’d purchased the food with uncharacteristic impulsiveness, but he didn’t think it would be wasted. “This food in exchange for you hearing it out .” “I…” She stared at the bundle, eyes gleaming . “Just listen to what I have to say.” He glanced up at the dark sky and wiped water away from his optics with his sleeve. “After we return to your dwelling .” “I don’t wa—” She snapped her mouth shut and dropped her gaze, brow furrowed. The hand holding the knife dropped to her side. “Fine. But I’m not promising anything beyond that .” Ronin nodded. “I only ask that you listen and give it fair consideration.” He tucked the food into a pocket inside his coat. Her eyes tracked it the entire way . “I’ll assist you through the debris.” He held his open hand to her . She glared at it, insult in her expression, and walked past him. “Don’t need your help,” she muttered . Shoulders squared, she shuffled forward, struggling through water now up to her mid-shin. When she stumbled, she waved her arms in wild circles, somehow maintaining her balance. Grumbling, she bent down and yanked off one of her boots. The sole flopped . Ronin frowned and scanned the road back to the human settlement. Rocks, chunks of concrete, and cracked asphalt were the least of her worries — the rising water hid countless hazards. Wood splinters, rusted nails, and shards of broken glass. Another open wound, especially in this dust-laden water, was too much of a risk . “It’s safer if I carry you .” “The hell you will.” She moved slowly, attention
